title: 4 Establishing Policies for Deposit Processing
body_text:
4	Establishing Policies for Deposit Processing
4.1	Overview of Policies for Deposit Processing
Financial institutions must comply with legal regulations that include, but arenât limited to, identification requirements, record-keeping requirements, and FINTRAC reporting requirements. Failure to comply can result in significant fines and/or imprisonment. Financial institutions must also comply with CPA regulations.
To ensure regulatory compliance and to mitigate risk, financial institutions must establish policies on accepting and processing items received on deposit.
4.2	Policy on Complying with PCMLTFA
To comply with
PCMLTFA
and
PCMLTF Regulations
, your financial institution must establish a policy on training staff who accept deposits
.
Subscribers to the Operations Manual Program can refer to the PCMLTFA Compliance Part for detailed information.
4.3	Policy on Obtaining the Payeeâs Endorsement
Itâs good practice to obtain the payeeâs endorsement on all cheques. However, CPA Rule A3, Endorsement and Identification of Paper Items, specifies that the payeeâs endorsement isnât mandatory when a cheque is deposited to a personâs account and that person is the same person as the named and intended payee on the cheque.
The rules for endorsement vary for cheques drawn on foreign financial institutions. The foreign financial institution may accept a âdeposited to the account ofâ stamp, or it may require the payee to endorse the cheque with the payeeâs name exactly as shown on the face of the cheque and with the payeeâs financial institution account number. If the cheque isnât endorsed as required, itâs returned. Therefore, obtain the payeeâs endorsement on all cheques that are drawn on foreign financial institutions.
Your financial institution can use the âDeposit to the Credit of the Payeeâ stamp for business customer deposits and fill in the business name and account number. Your financial institution or business can have a stamp that contains the businessâs name and account number.
Your financial institution can establish a policy that requires all cheques to be endorsed with the payeeâs name exactly as shown on the face of the cheque and with the payeeâs financial institution account number.
Related Information
CPA Rule A3, Endorsement and Identification of Paper Items
4.4	Policy on Accepting US Treasury Cheques
We recommend that your financial institution establishes policies on accepting cheques issued by the US Treasury. If your financial institution chooses to accept US Treasury cheques, careful consideration should be given to your customerâs relationship with your financial institution and to the amount of the cheque presented for payment.
Cheques issued by the US Treasury donât follow regular time constraints for returned items. These cheques can be returned by the US Government (e.g., for fraud) up to one year from the time of clearing, and there is no recourse for your financial institution.
These cheques can be verified with the US Treasury if a valid routing number, cheque number, and amount are provided. See Section 1.2, Contacts, Websites, and Other Resources, for US Treasury Check Information System (TCIS) contact information.
4.5	Policy on Accepting Deposits for Collection
Your financial institution must establish a policy on whether it accepts cheques when settlement can only be obtained from the drawee branch via the collections process. Financial institutions arenât required to accept cheques that must be sent on collection.
If your financial institution does accept cheques that are sent on collection, Central 1 doesnât recommend accepting a foreign funds cheque of $300 (Canadian equivalent) or less because the pass-through charges can be substantial. However, your financial institution can set the minimum dollar value according to your internal policy.
4.6	Policy on Depositing On-Us Items
A cheque deposited to an account held at a financial institution that is drawn on an account held at the same financial institution is known as an on-us item.
Central 1 recommends that financial institutions deposit on-us items to Central 1 with their other deposit items. Although these cheques arenât exchanged through the clearing, they are otherwise treated the same as cheques drawn on other financial institutions.
If your financial institution doesnât deposit on-us items to Central 1, these items
wonât be included in the image file for customersâ statements;
wonât appear in ORS, in which case your financial institution must maintain its own image archive and storage of physical items for research and returns;
wonât appear on Central 1âs deposit reports, clearing reports, or settlement files; and
wonât be processed in the same manner as other deposits or incoming clearing items, in which case your financial institution must maintain two processes for deposits and incoming clearing items and must train staff accordingly.
4.7	Policy on Using Mobile Deposits
Itâs your financial institutionâs responsibility to establish policies and procedures on managing risk for Mobile Deposits. Financial institutions can set a maximum deposit amount and a maximum number of deposits per day or week by customer. Financial institutions can also establish policies on determining which customers use Mobile Deposits. As with other products, âknow your customerâ before enabling Mobile Deposit functionality.
4.8	Policy on Corporate Capture
Itâs your financial institutionâs responsibility to establish policies and procedures on managing risk for Corporate Capture deposits. Financial institutions can choose to receive real-time messages of Corporate Capture deposits. These real-time messages can be used within a financial institutionâs banking host to set rules regarding the deposits made by business customers (e.g., maximum deposit amount and a maximum number of deposits per day or week). Financial institutions can also establish policies on determining which business customers can use Corporate Capture. As with other products, âknow your customerâ before enabling Corporate Capture functionality.
4.9	Policy on Retention and Destruction of Items Processed via Remote Deposit Capture
Your financial institution must establish a policy for retaining and destroying payment items deposited via Remote Deposit Capture. This currently includes Mobile Deposits, Branch Capture deposits, and Corporate Capture deposits. Central 1 recommends that your financial institution retain all physical items captured via Remote Deposit Capture in a secure location and restrict access to the physical items until they are destroyed securely.
CPA Rule A10, Image Rule, specifies that payment items deposited by image exchange must be destroyed within 120 days of deposit. The CPA doesnât stipulate a minimum retention period. However, items with poor image quality can be dishonoured up to 90 days after clearing.
For Branch Capture, Central 1 recommends that your financial institution retain physical items for 90 days in case an item needs to be rescanned. Most poor-quality images are detected within a few days of depositing the items, so some financial institutions may elect to have a shorter retention period.
For Mobile Deposits and Corporate Capture, itâs important for your financial institution to clearly communicate your destruction policy to your customers. Specifically, you must inform customers of the time frame for retaining physical cheques and provide guidance on how the cheques should be destroyed.
Note:	If a cheque is destroyed and it wasnât imaged and deposited correctly, itâs the customer's responsibility to obtain a replacement cheque from the payor.
4.10 	Policy on Restricting Access to the Branch Capture and Corporate Capture Applications
It is recommended that policies relating to the use of the Branch Capture and Corporate Capture applications be established to prevent or detect errors in a timely manner and to restrict access to the Branch Capture and Corporate Capture applications. Such policies can include, but arenât limited to:
Limiting access to the Branch Capture and Corporate Capture applications to individuals who need the application to complete their job requirements
Ensuring that the Branch Capture or the Corporate Capture user type assigned to individuals accurately reflects their current job responsibilities and authorization levels
Determining whether individuals can be set up with multiple Branch Capture or Corporate Capture user types
Note:	Individuals typically have access to Branch Capture or Corporate Capture as one user type. This ensures that deposit processing duties are separated. However, your financial institution can assign an individual to multiple user types according to your internal policy.
Training individuals not to share user IDs
Prohibiting the use of multiple user IDs for an individual
Identifying and deactivating inactive Branch Capture and/or Corporate Capture application users
4.11	Policy on Duplicate Items
Financial institutions submitting paper deposit items to Central 1 via Branch Capture and/or Corporate Capture are responsible for establishing policies and procedures on managing potential duplicate items. Duplicate detection is provided on a best-efforts basis and isnât guaranteed.
Branch Capture and Corporate capture identify items as potential duplicates if an itemâs MICR and amount read during scanning matches the MICR and amount of an item that was previously scanned. Items identified as potential duplicates arenât necessarily actual duplicates; false positives can occur from misreads or from items with incomplete MICR (such as counter cheques). Users must carefully review items identified as potential duplicates to determine how to process them.
Branch Capture or Corporate Capture Users must follow their financial institutionâs policies regarding overriding potential duplicate item warnings and transmitting the items for processing. If you delete an item identified as a potential duplicate from a deposit, notify your customer.
4.12	Policy on Client Centre Passwords
Branch Capture
Branch Capture users access the system through a password login to the Client Centre. Once the user is successfully authenticated, the Branch Capture option appears in the userâs Applications list. Secure passwords are necessary to prevent unauthorized access to the computer network. See the CPS Admin Application Part for more information.
Corporate Capture
Corporate Capture users who are business customers access the system through a password login via the Corporate Capture URL. Corporate Capture users who are financial institution staff can access the system either through the Corporate Capture URL or through the Client Centre.
If the Corporate Capture user (business customer or financial institution staff) accesses the system via the Corporate Capture URL, theyâll be redirected to the Corporate Capture site.
If the Corporate Capture user (financial institution staff only) accesses the system via the Client Centre, the Branch Capture option appears in the userâs Applications list. Since Corporate Capture shares common attributes with Branch Capture, financial institution staff who are Corporate Capture users can access Corporate Capture from the Branch Capture application link.
Secure passwords are necessary to prevent unauthorized access to the computer network. See the CPS Admin Application Part for more information.
4.13	Policy on Accepting Euro and Pound Sterling Deposit Items
Central 1 doesnât accept Euro and Pound Sterling deposit items via paper exchange or via Branch Capture or Corporate Capture. Your financial institution needs to develop internal policies for handling Euro and Pound Sterling deposit items. These items can be sent on collection, or you can have the customer request an incoming wire instead.
END
